On a P/E of 7.1x, Fuji Seal International looks inexpensive next to both its own fair P/E estimate and the wider packaging peer group, while the last close sits at ¥2,920.

The P/E multiple compares the share price to earnings per share, so you are essentially asking how much the market is paying for each unit of profit. According to the statements, the shares are considered good value based on their P/E of 7.1x compared to an estimated fair P/E of 10.1x. Compared with the JP Packaging industry average P/E of 9.6x and a peer average of 11.6x, the current 7.1x stands out as clearly lower. data.title: Assessing CARsgen Therapeutics Holdings’ Valuation After New CAR T-Cell Manufacturing Cooperation News
data.description:
CARsgen Therapeutics Holdings (SEHK:2171) has signed cooperation agreements via its subsidiary CARsgen Diagnostics with Shanghai Jingong Enterprise to build a commercial CAR T-cell manufacturing base in Shanghai, a move closely linked to its commercialization plans.

See our latest analysis for CARsgen Therapeutics Holdings.

The cooperation news comes as momentum in CARsgen Therapeutics Holdings' share price has recently picked up, with a 1-day share price return of 4.65% and a 7-day share price return of 8.50%. However, the 90-day share price return of 15.76% contrasts with a stronger 1-year total shareholder return of 33.60%, suggesting that recent enthusiasm is building on a more mixed medium term.

If you are watching CAR T-cell names, it could also be worth seeing what else is moving in related areas through our screener of 106 healthcare AI stocks as another set of ideas to research.

With CARsgen investing up to RMB 370 million in new capacity and the share price still below the average analyst target, some investors may question whether the stock is undervalued at this level or whether the market is already pricing in future growth.

Advertisement

Preferred Price-to-Book of 7.7x: Is it justified?

At a last close of HK$15.07, CARsgen Therapeutics Holdings is trading on a P/B of 7.7x, which screens as expensive against both its industry and peer averages.

P/B compares the company’s market value to its accounting book value. It is often used for early stage or loss-making biotech businesses where earnings are not yet a stable guide. For CARsgen, this means investors are currently paying a multiple of its net assets that is higher than many other Hong Kong listed biotechs.

That premium sits against a mixed backdrop. Earnings are currently in loss, with reduced losses over the past 5 years and forecasts indicating the company is expected to become profitable within 3 years and revenue is forecast to grow faster than the wider Hong Kong market. At the same time, there is insufficient data to run the SWS DCF model on its future cash flows, so there is no cross-check from a cash flow based valuation.

The comparison with peers is stark. CARsgen’s 7.7x P/B is higher than the Hong Kong Biotechs industry average of 4.4x and also above the peer average of 5.1x. This suggests the market is assigning a premium price tag relative to similar names. For investors, that raises a clear question: whether the current P/B multiple is paying in advance for future growth that has yet to appear in the financials.

See what the numbers say about this price — find out in our valuation breakdown.

Result: Price-to-book of 7.7x (OVERVALUED)

However, you still need to weigh risks such as ongoing net losses of CN¥522.057 million and a 3-year total shareholder return decline of 12.59%.
